text: Catalytic dinitrogen reduction remains a key focus area in the search for low energy ammonia synthesis methods. Here we report the synthesis and catalytic investigation of a range of molybdenum precursors probed by N-heterocyclic carbene-based PCP-type pincer ligands toward ambient pressure ammonia synthesis. These investigations utilized the established $SmI_2\cdot nH_2O$ reducing environment and resulting ammonia yields at varying catalyst concentrations suggested a redox-dependent transition from catalyst activation to the rate determining steps of the reaction cycle. At high catalyst concentration the rate determining step was found to be the proton coupled electron transfer step during the N–H bonding to the molybdenum imide. This is switched to catalyst dimerization at lower concentrations. Aiming to modulate the rate determining activation energy for ammonia synthesis, DFT studies pointed toward the utilization of electron withdrawing groups on the benzimidazole of the pincer ligand to facilitate a higher LUMO energy for the nitride for catalyst activation while also increasing the Mo–C(carbene) bond strength. Substituted pincer complexes along with the parent $MoCI_3\left(PCP\right)$ complex precursor were synthesized and catalytic investigations of the performance of these derivatives indicated a trifluoromethyl-group containing $MoCI_3$ complex-derived catalyst to give the highest overall performance to date, namely a TO NH3 of 60,000 and a TOF of 800.
